chapters.summary: Majority Leader Shaun Abreu opens the July 16, 2026 stated meeting, welcomes the public, and reminds attendees that only council members may speak during stated meetings. He notes the agenda is available online.
chapters.title: Majority Leader Abreu opens stated meeting and reminds decorum

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Stated meeting of July 16, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I am Majority Leader Shaun-Abreu, and I'd like to thank you for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: As a reminder, decorum must be respected at all times.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: If you would like to follow along, the agenda for today's meeting is posted on our website.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: On behalf of the body, I welcome the members of the public who have joined us today.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: During stated meetings, only members of the council may speak.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Members of the public shall remain silent.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: If you wish to express your support of any remarks, you may make the silent approval gesture.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore of New Jerusalem Worship Center in Jamaica, Queens delivers a brief invocation praying for wisdom and guidance for the council members.
chapters.title: Invocation by Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: We will now have today's invocation, which will be delivered by the Reverend Dr.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Gamal Delezbore of New Jerusalem Worship Center, which is located at 122-05 Smith Street in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Let us pray.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Oh, Lord, our Lord, how excellent is your name in all the earth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Father God, we just thank you for allowing us to be here one more time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Lord God, as we convene and we look to you, the author and finisher of our faith, Father God, bless every heart and mind that is here within this council meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Lead them and guide them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: According to your wisdom, knowledge, and understanding, anoint them afresh, Lord, as they look to you, Lord God, as you begin to just show yourself mighty, show yourself strong in the midst.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: We thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: We praise you, giving you all the honor, glory, and praise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: In the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore
utterances.text: Amen.

chapters.summary: Council Member Ty Hankerson moves to spread the invocation in full on the record and delivers a detailed biographical tribute to Rev. Dr. Gamel Delesbore, covering his Queens roots, spiritual journey, theological training, community service, and family life.
chapters.title: Hankerson spreads invocation on record, honors Rev. Delesbore's biography

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: That was very brief, huh?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I would now ask Councilmember Ty Hankerson to spread the invocation on the record.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: We get things done.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: I would like to thank Reverend Gamal Delispor for being here today, and I make a motion for unanimous consent to spread the invocation in full upon the record.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Reverend Dr.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Gamal A.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Delispor is a lifelong New Yorker from Hollis, Queens, shaped by the strong community and church roots of his upbringing.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Raised by his mother, the late Grace Marie Branch Delispor, organist at Cornerstone Baptist Church in Brooklyn, he learned early the value of service, faith, and community leadership.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: His spiritual journey deepened in 1990 when he came to faith in Christ at Long Island City Gospel Tabernacle under Bishop Moses Taylor.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: He pursued biblical training at the Long Island City Bible Institute, earning a certificate in evangelism in 1995, and later answered his call to ministry in 2004.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Licensed in 2005 at New Missionary Baptist Church under Reverend Larry L.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Schell, he served the youth of his community for four years, investing in the next generation.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Reverend Delisboer continued his development through New York Theological Seminary and later through advanced academic study, earning degrees from Alliance Theological Seminary, Regent University, and Newburgh Theological Seminary.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: At the New Jerusalem Worship Center, he has served in numerous roles that strengthen both the

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: congregation and the surrounding community, including spiritual development coordinator, evangelism ministry, brotherhood ministry, transportation ministry, and personal safety and security for Bishop and Lady Rice.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: His leadership is grounded in accessibility, compassion, and a commitment to community well-being.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Beyond the church, he serves as a community health worker in the IN, also known as InTouch Institute,

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: and as an educator with the New York City Department of Education at the New Heights Charter School of Harlem, continuing his lifelong commitment to empowering individuals and families.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Reverend Dr.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Gamal A.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Delispor and his wife of 34 years, Crystal Denise Jones Delispor, are proud parents and grandparents, deeply rooted in faith, family, and community, and we thank him for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Speaker Julie Menin reports on yesterday's 10-hour hearing on Romange's Law, named after Romange Mahajan who died after being thrown from a horse-drawn carriage in Central Park. She thanks Council Member Shulman and all who attended, and notes the Council will consider amendments as the process continues.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on Romange's Law hearing

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, on this very hot and hazy day.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So this has been a very busy week, to put it mildly, for the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yesterday we held a hearing on Romange's Law, named after Romange Mahajan, who tragically died after he was thrown from a horse-drawn carriage in Central Park last month.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We heard very compelling testimony from his family, from animal welfare advocates, from TWU, from drivers, medallion owners.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I really, first of all, want to thank Councilmember Shulman for sharing that marathon.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I think it was 10 hours it clocked in at the end.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 10-Hour hearing and all the council members who turned out, and I look forward to hearing thoughts from additional council members as we go through this process and as we consider any amendments and additional language to the bill.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And thank you to Council Member Marte as well.

chapters.summary: Menin introduces a bill she sponsored establishing a quadrennial commission to ease permitting, licensing, and inspections for small businesses, noting 5,000 closures last year. She also highlights legislation by Council Member Zhuang to cut bureaucratic red tape, sharing that some business owners wait years to open.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on small business legislation

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, so moving along, for those residents who are small business owners, we've kept up our efforts to make life easier for them.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Today we're introducing a bill that I sponsored.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet on the floor, please.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That aims to topple the barriers that prevent small businesses from succeeding.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This bill would establish a quadrennial commission whose recommendations would ease permitting and licensing and inspections, as well as reduce fines and fees.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know that about 5,000 small businesses closed last year, so this is an urgent, urgent crisis.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: In that vein, we're voting on legislation sponsored by Council Member Zhuang, which would help cut through the maze of bureaucratic red tape that aspiring business owners are forced to navigate.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As a former small business owner myself, I really understand that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We talked to so many small business owners and heard from them, some of whom said it's taking them sometimes several years to be able to open their small business.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that's not acceptable, and now the council is taking action to address that.

chapters.summary: Menin announces the Council secured $1.5 million in the budget for 60,000 free swim lessons for an additional 3,000 second graders, building on a pilot program with the Gray Foundation and Asphalt Green that already served 6,000 students. She notes the announcement at Lehman College in the Bronx.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on $1.5M for free swim lessons

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: On Tuesday, we announced that the council secured $1.5 million in the budget for

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 60,000 free swim lessons for an additional 3,000 second graders.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This was so exciting, and I was proud to be at Lehman College in the Bronx with a number of colleagues where we announced this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I think this is so incredibly important.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It builds on the work we did from the last council.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I had had a bill that mandated free swim lessons to every single second grader, but in the last administration that wasn't fully implemented.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So now we're adding additional funding to do that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And this builds on some work that I had done with Councilmember Krishnan and the Gray Foundation and Asphalt Green, where we launched a pilot program about a year ago, which has already provided free swim lessons to 6,000 public school second graders.

chapters.summary: Menin advocates for paraprofessionals and the Respect Act, noting starting salary of $32,500, 1,600 DOE vacancies, and $1.5 billion spent on Carter cases. She shares that some paras live in shelters and cannot afford groceries, calling it unconscionable and urging passage of legislation to address it.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on paraprofessional pay crisis

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Earlier this morning, I was thrilled to gather in the rotunda with many of you where we advocated for our paraprofessionals and for the Respect Act.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Proud to be wearing this pin on this today for our amazing, amazing paras.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I first of all want to thank Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: She and I have been on a two-year journey along with the UFT on this important work.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And I just want to say a word about this.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Our city's paraprofessional starting salary is $32,500 to be specific.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We know our paras are helping our most special resource, our children, and really our most vulnerable children, those with special needs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We have 1,600 vacancies at DOE of paraprofessionals.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We are spending $1.5 billion a year on Carter cases, largely because in so many classrooms there is no paraprofessional.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So many of our paraprofessionals, and we've heard this literally at every single rally, I think we did four rallies,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Councilmember DeRosa, you and I on this issue, and at every rally we hear from paraprofessionals who literally say some of them are living in the shelter system, some cannot afford groceries.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This is unconscionable.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so today, our fervent hope is that we will finally pass legislation to address it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I want to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Obviously, we believe this should be done via collective bargaining or through the budget, but that was not happening.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so we, the council, had to act.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so I think this is just incredibly, incredibly important that we finally, once and for all, give paraprofessionals the pay they so rightly deserve.

chapters.summary: Menin wishes early birthdays to Council Members Justin Sanchez (July 17), Elsie Encarnación (July 19), and Rita Joseph and Tiffany Cabán (July 24). She also notes excitement for the Spain-Argentina World Cup final at MetLife Stadium and the colorful international visitors across the five boroughs.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin recognizes birthdays and World Cup excitement

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: On a sweeter note, there are a few things to look forward to.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I want to first of all wish a very happy early birthday to Councilmember Justin Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: His birthday is July 17th.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: A happy early birthday to Deputy Whip Elsie Encarnación.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Her birthday is on the 19th, so in a couple days.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: To Council Members Rita Joseph and Tiffany Cabán, whose birthday is on July 24th.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So happy, happy birthday.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This Sunday, I think we're all going to be excitedly watching as Spain and Argentina face off in the World Cup finals right across the river at MetLife Stadium.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Over the past few weeks, we've been proud to continue the long practice of welcoming visitors from all across the world to our great city, seeing their colorful jerseys, hearing the mix of languages all across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that has been just so incredibly exciting to see.

chapters.summary: Menin marks July 24th as National Disabilities Day, commemorating the 36th anniversary of the Americans with Disabilities Act. She recalls the Capitol steps protest by nearly 60 activists and calls on the Council to continue their effort to make the city more equal for all.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on ADA 36th anniversary

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: July 24th is National Disabilities Day, marking the 36th anniversary of the American with Disabilities Act.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We remember the decades of activism it took for Congress to pass this landmark legislation, including when nearly 60 activists ditched their mobility aids, dragged their bodies up the Capitol steps, and demanded their rights.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thanks to their hard work, the Court of Americans who are disabled are guaranteed the exact same rights to work, for transit, and for access to space as everyone else.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And as a council, we must continue their effort to make our nation more equal for all.

chapters.summary: Menin welcomes the Consul General of Lithuania and Lithuanian Alliance members, noting New York's history as an entry point for Lithuanian immigrants. She also welcomes interns from Senator Chuck Schumer's office and students from the Bronx Scholars Institute at Hostos College.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin welcomes Lithuanian delegation, Schumer interns, and Bronx students

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We have a few special guests in our chambers that I'd like to give a very warm welcome to.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First, the Council General of Lithuania, as well as the Lithuanian Alliance members and associates.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Welcome.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Welcome.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we know New York and Lithuania have shared a long history together, which goes back to when our city was the entry point.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: point for thousands of Lithuanian immigrants.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we really welcome you here today.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And another welcome, we have the interns from Senator Chuck Schumer's office.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we want to welcome you here to the chambers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then lastly, students from the Bronx Scholars Institute at Hustos College, we welcome you as well.

chapters.summary: Menin outlines the day's land use votes, which will facilitate 3,242 new housing units across all five boroughs with nearly 1,700 affordable. She thanks Land Use Chair Kevin Riley and details projects including Monitor Point (1,324 units, 50% affordable), DeWitt Clinton Park North (1,064 units), 2950 West 24th Street (408 units), 1166 Bedford Avenue (144 senior units), 198-208 Richmond Terrace (118 units), 1160 Pugsley Avenue (104 units), and 164th Street (80 units).
chapters.title: Speaker Menin overview of land use applications: 3,242 new housing units

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, we have a busy stated agenda.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First, we're going to vote on a number of land use items.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Today, this council is taking essential action to address our city's housing affordability crisis by approving land use applications that together

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: are going to facilitate a total of 3,242 units of new housing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Through a combination of HPD subsidy and mandatory inclusionary housing, over half of this housing will be affordable.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That's nearly 1,700 units.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I really want to thank our land use chair, Councilmember Kevin Riley, for shepherding these projects through and for your leadership.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These applications span across all five boroughs, and the council's negotiations prioritize adding and deepening affordable housing, especially at Monitor Point, where the project we are voting on today represents an increase of over 200

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: affordable units from what was presented just weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This council will continue to proactively work to deliver new homes as well as community benefits for New Yorkers as we move forward this term.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Monitor Point, and I was really happy to partner with Councilmember Ressler on this very, very important project,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: an application to facilitate the development of two new mixed-use buildings with approximately 1,324 residential units to be exact, 50% of which will be affordable.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As well as a new museum honoring the legacy of the USS Monitor, as well as publicly accessible open space in Councilmember Ressler's district.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The applicant team is committed to contributing $300,000 annually to the long-term maintenance of Bushwick Inlet Park and facilitating the relocation of two MTA facilities.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: DeWitt Clinton Park North, two related applications in Councilmember Gale-A-Brewer's district to facilitate the redevelopment of two auto dealerships into mixed-use towers with a combined 1,064 units,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 286 of which will be affordable under MIH.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: As part of the applications, the developers will also be purchasing air rights.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: From Hudson River Park, helping to generate important and critical funding for waterfront open space.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And next is 2950 West 24th Street rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This would facilitate the development of an 18-story mixed-use building with 408 units, all of which will be income restricted pursuant to HPD's ELA program,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: as well as two superintendent units, commercial community facility space,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Councilmember Kayla Santosuoso's district.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 1166 Bedford Avenue rezoning will facilitate the development of a 12-story mixed-use building with approximately 144

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: affordable independent residences for seniors, as well as ground floor retail and community facility space in Councilmember Chi-A.-Ossé's district.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 198-208 Richmond Terrace will facilitate the development of a 14-story mixed-use building with 118

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: residential units, 35 of which will be permanently affordable under MIH, as well as community facility space and off-street accessory parking in Majority Whip Kamillah-Hanks District.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: 1160 Pugsley Avenue rezoning will facilitate the development of a new eight-story mixed-use building with approximately 104 residential units and ground floor supermarket space in Councilmember Amanda Farias'district.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then lastly, 164th Street rezoning will facilitate the development of a new

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: six-story mixed-use building with 80 residential units, 24 of which will be affordable under MIH and community facility space in Councilmember Jim Gennaro's district.

chapters.summary: Menin covers LPC designations of three immigration-related landmarks (PS 15 Annex, Church of St. Mary, Lithuanian Alliance building), an 801-seat high school at 25 Wall Street, and two HPD applications for preserving existing affordable housing at 511 West 171st Street and East Harlem El Barrio.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on landmark designations, school siting, and affordable housing preservation

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Next, Landmarks Preservation Commission designation of three individual landmarks linked to New York City's immigration history.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First, the Public School 15 Annex, located in Councilmember Lincoln-Ressler's district, originally built in 1889 to serve Brooklyn's growing immigrant community.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Second, the Church of St.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Mary in Councilmember Chris Marte's district, which is the third oldest Roman Catholic church in all of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then finally, the Lithuanian Alliance building in Councilmember Carl Wilson's district, which has served as a home for the Lithuanian Alliance of America since 1910.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We are also approving a school siting in Majority Whip Camilla Hanks District for a new approximately 801-seat high school at 25 Wall Street.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then finally, two applications by HPD to facilitate the preservation of existing affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First is 511 West 171st Street, ANCP Cluster.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: To facilitate the rehabilitation of three city-owned buildings with 50 units under the Affordable Neighborhood Cooperative Program in Councilmember Carmen-N.-De-La-Rosa's district.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then next, the East Harlem-El Barrio Article 11 Amendment to a project previously approved by the Council in 2019

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to allow commercial spaces to be occupied by community facility uses in Deputy Whip Elsie-Encarnación and Councilmember Yusef-Salaam's districts.

chapters.summary: Menin outlines finance items including a transparency resolution approving new designations for expense budget funding and a pre-considered introduction by Council Member Linda Lee decreasing the city personal income tax credit for residents' share of unincorporated business tax paid by partnerships.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on finance items: transparency resolution and UBT credit decrease

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We will also be voting on the following finance items, a transparency resolution approving new designations and changes of certain organizations receiving funding in the expense budget.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered introductions sponsored by

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Councilmember Linda Lee would decrease the credit against the city personal income tax on residents for their share of city unincorporated business tax, also known as a UBT,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: paid by partnerships in which such residents are partners or businesses of which they are proprietors.

chapters.summary: Menin previews Resolution 371 by Council Member Salaam declaring August 6 as Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day, Intro 398 by Council Member Restler amending annual disclosure law, and Intro 913A by Council Member Farias raising the NYPD civil service exam age limit from 35 to 43.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on Resolution 371, Intro 398, and Intro 913A

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Today, we are also going to vote on the following pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Resolution 371, sponsored by Councilmember Yusef-Salaam, would declare August 6th annually as Fat Man's Group Recognition Day in the City of New York.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Alejandro Caraval and Regina Paul.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 398, sponsored by Councilmember Lincoln Ressler, would amend the annual disclosure law to affect the bans a New York City officer, employee, candidate for office, or political party official selects

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: when disclosing anything with a monetary value or amount.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Jahari Frazier and Erica Cohen.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 913A, sponsored by Councilmember Amanda Faddeus, would raise the maximum age at which someone can take the civil service exam to become a police officer from 35 years of age to 43 years of age.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This legislation would align local eligibility standards with those established pursuant to state law.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Rachel Codrero, David Seitzer, Josh Kingsley, and Chad Benjamin.

chapters.summary: Menin covers Intro 812A by Encarnación requiring wellness checks on older adults during weather emergencies, Intro 496A by Williams requiring a study on acknowledging the 1863 draft riots, and a pre-considered introduction increasing elected official salaries by 18.2% per the Quadrennial Advisory Commission report.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on Intro 812A, Intro 496A, and elected official compensation

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 812A, sponsored by Deputy Whip Elsie-Encarnación, would require that

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Commissioner of the Department for the Aging to develop a protocol in collaboration with New York City Emergency Management and relevant agencies to conduct wellness checks

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: during cold weather alerts, extreme heat warnings like we have now, and heat-related emergencies.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Hannah Cohn and Joshua Newman.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 496A, sponsored by Deputy Speaker Dr.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Nantasha Williams, would require the Commissioner of Cultural Affairs to conduct a study and provide a report to the Mayor as well as the Council on the potential installation of structures acknowledging the giraffe riots of 1863.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Tristan Monteith and Alejandro Caravelle.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Pre-considered introduction A, also sponsored by Dr.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Nantasha Williams, would increase the salaries of the mayor, public advocate, controller, council member, borough presidents, and district attorneys by 18.2%

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: since the last time their salaries were adjusted by local law, as recommended by the recently released report of the New York City Quadrennial Advisory Commission for the Review of Compensation Levels of Elected Officials.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It would change the timing of future commissions to the third year of a term of office rather than the first year and provide each commission with 120 days to complete their work.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Jahari Frazier and Erica Cohen.

chapters.summary: Menin outlines Intro 955A by Council Member Zhuang requiring coordinated inspections and plan reviews to help businesses open faster, and Intro 692A by Council Member De La Rosa providing up to $10,000 in workforce stabilization payments to eligible school paraprofessionals in four installments.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on Intro 955A Red Tape Relief Act and Intro 692A para stabilization

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Introduction 955-A, also known as a red tape.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Relief Act, sponsored by Councilmember Susan Zhuang, would require the mayor to establish a program that would coordinate inspections and plan reviews among agencies to cut down on the time for new

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: businesses to open.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to David Seiser, Tyler Walls, and Rebecca Barilla.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And finally, introduction 692-A, sponsored by Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa, would require the Department of Education to provide a workforce stabilization payment totaling up to $10,000

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to each eligible school paraprofessional for work performed during the 2026-2027 school year.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Such workforce stabilization payment would be paid to eligible school paraprofessionals in four separate installments.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you to Rhi, Ogasawara, Elliot Lin, and Samita Deshmukh.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So thank you for your attention to that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'll turn it back over to our Majority Leader.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer discusses the 629 West 54th Street and 801 11th Avenue applications in her district, noting 1,064 units with 286 affordable under MIH. She highlights $29.7 million for Hudson River Park Trust, concerns about park shadows, the need for more affordable housing in Manhattan, and the developers' dependence on 485X.
chapters.title: Brewer on DeWitt Clinton Park North development

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, you're first on the list.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to talk about the 629 West 54th Street and 801 11th Avenue, which we'll be voting on today.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 87 or a little bit more are there affordable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There are some that are very affordable as part of MI8, and then we negotiate another 12 to 14 at a higher AMI.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's still challenging.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We wanted some workforce housing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We got just a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These developers are contributing $200,000 to a nearby park, but I have to be honest with you, the park needs $12 million, to be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's a little bit lower in terms of height, these buildings, but we're worried about the shadow on the park.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Luckily, there are car dealerships with good paying jobs, union jobs that will be in the buildings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The most interesting part, as the speaker mentioned, is that $29.7 million at least will be given to the Hudson River Park Trust.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It will hopefully not only does increase FAR, obviously, of the buildings, that's the purpose of it, but it will also help the trust pay for pilings under Pier 76, which was the toll pound for Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to say that because of the length of time allowed to construct up to 10 years as part of this permit, I want to make sure that the Hudson River Park Trust enters into an agreement with the developers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They want to make sure it's binding to this particular development because even if alternative becomes available later, we want to make sure that the money goes to the trust and the promise is the promise.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Board 4, particularly Joe Resticcia and Paul Devlin and the Land Use Division of the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to say the developers also say they're not going to build if they get a no amendment to 485X.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know where that's going to go, but they are obviously very concerned about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have to say when you're on private land and you're in Manhattan, I wish the city would give us more, either some vouchers or some other ways of having more units be affordable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And there should be a 30% of the entire square footage, not 25 or 30% of the number of units.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Manhattan has transportation, we have good schools, and darn it, we should be able to get more affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I appreciate that they go to other boroughs because they all say it costs less on terms of the purchase.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I thank everybody involved and just wish we could do more affordable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler, followed by Santos Suoso.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler expresses support for the 40 Quay Street Monitor Point project, noting affordable housing tripled from 247 to 662 units (50% of total), with deeply affordable units for seniors and 110 supportive housing units. He highlights 50,000 square feet of green space, $300,000 annual park maintenance contributions, ADA-accessible G train elevator, and MTA facility relocation.
chapters.title: Restler on Monitor Point development in Greenpoint

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am pleased to express my support today for the proposed development at 40 Quay Street in Greenpoint, also known as Monitor Point.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Since this project was first proposed about five years ago, I have been clear that any development on publicly owned land must be primarily in the public interest.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'm grateful for the thousands, I mean that literally, thousands of neighbors who've reached out to me and to my team to share their perspective over the course of this process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: In direct response to our feedback, the developer has nearly tripled the amount of affordable housing, permanently affordable housing, that will be built on this site, from originally about 247 units to now 600.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 62 affordable units, 50% of the total development.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think many of us and my community members for sure are jaded when we hear the term affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is it actually affordable?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And in this case, I can say yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: A majority of the housing is deeply affordable.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm talking about a family of three making $76,300 per year will be able to rent a two-bedroom at $18,000.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We are building 161 housing units that are affordable for seniors.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That means a senior making $35,000 a year will be paying $900 a month.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And 30% of the affordable units, 110 in total, will be supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Those are for people exiting our shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I pushed for even more, but I'm grateful that we were able to secure so many supportive units.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This development will also yield significant green space benefits for our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The developer will be adding over 50,000 square feet of green space.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The developer will be contributing $300,000 per year for 99 years toward the maintenance of Bushwick Inlet Park, the adjacent park space.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There will be two new public bathrooms that are accessible to park users and to neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And the MTA is relocating a facility from the northern tip of Greenpoint where we will be now able to fully build out Box Street Park, one of the other promised parks from the Williamsburg Greenpoint rezoning 20 years ago when we break ground later this summer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're getting a new, I know I'm done.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're getting a new ADA accessible G train with an elevator.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: G-Train, thank you to Speaker Menon for being a tremendous partner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Kevin Riley, our land use chair, the land use team, the MTA, the Gotham, and others.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate you all.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And Clement Oyster.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kayla Santosuosso speaks in support of the 2950 West 24th Street development bringing 408 permanently affordable units to Coney Island. She emphasizes that affordable housing must also be quality housing, and shares that the developer has promised clean, dignified living conditions with prompt repairs.
chapters.title: Santosuosso on 2950 West 24th Street development in Coney Island

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I now have Council Member Santosu also online.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Today we are voting to approve new development at 2950 West 24th Street.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This development will bring 400 units that will be permanently and deeply affordable, as well as shared green space for tenants and ground floor commercial space, all of which is desperately needed in Coney Island.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This building will be located on a lot already owned by the developer and where there is already a deeply affordable building on the site that is totally full.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So clearly a reflection that this building is needed.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We have had productive conversations with the developer to ensure that these new units are of the quality that residents deserve and that both tenants of the existing building and the new tenants see that any required repairs and maintenance be done quickly.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I strongly believe that affordable housing is important, but affordable doesn't mean that it can't be quality housing.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And accordingly, the developer has promised our office to ensure that these buildings are clean and dignified places to live, and I will be working with him and with tenants to ensure that this promise is upheld.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This spot is a prime example of how we can retrofit space to expand our affordable housing stock and do so in a way that expands amenities for current residents.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Eight units means thousands of individuals and families can find stability of permanently affordable homes.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And the proposal before us demonstrates our commitment to address the ongoing housing crisis with bold, smart development.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And I am proud to vote yes and encourage my colleagues to do so.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shirley Aldebol discloses her brother-in-law works for the Department for the Aging. As Civil Service and Labor Chair, she explains her support for Intro 692A, calling paraprofessionals vital to public education and arguing the non-pensionable stabilization payment does not interfere with collective bargaining. She votes yes on all items.
chapters.title: Aldebol explains vote, discloses brother-in-law at DFTA, supports 692A

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Alibol.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I want to disclose that my brother-in-law works for the Department for the Aging, and I'd like permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: As the chair of the Committee on Civil Service and Labor, I am excited to vote yes for 692A.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Paraprofessionals are vital to public education.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Councilmember De La Rosa's bill takes a significant first step towards fixing the persistent paraprofessional staffing crisis that has been impacting New York City schools.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Paraprofessionals are critical members of our school environment, providing instructional, behavioral, and physical support to students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: This is an important first step towards recognizing the true value of our city's paraprofessionals, providing them with some reprieve and incentivizing recruitment and retention.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Just want to be very clear, I have a deep respect for the collective bargaining process.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: This bill's non-pensionable workforce stabilization payment structure does not interfere, in my view, with mandatory subjects of collective bargaining, as it does not attempt to legislate wages or benefits and matters that must be resolved.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: at the bargaining table.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Congratulations to my colleague, Councilmember Carmen de la Rosa, for her leadership on this bill and for her dedication to our city's power professionals and the students and families who depend on them.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And thank you, Speaker Manin, for moving this bill to vote on the floor today.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I vote yes on all.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff

utterances.text: collectiveness for our EMS workers.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And I should have done this first, Majority Leader, I'm sorry, but I disclose that my son works for the DSNY.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.text: Banks.

chapters.summary: Council Member Chris Banks discloses a brother at NYC Parks Department and another at Department of Juvenile Justice. He delivers an extended statement supporting Intro 692A, describing paraprofessionals as hardworking New Yorkers who support the most vulnerable students, and arguing the one-time payment recognizes their commitment while broader bargaining continues.
chapters.title: Banks explains vote on 692A, discloses family at Parks and Juvenile Justice

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I would like to disclose first that I have a brother that works for the New York City Parks Department and also a brother that works for the Department of Juvenile Justice.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: First of all, thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And first, I want to again thank the Speaker and my good, good colleague, Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: for ushering this particular bill, the Paraprofessional Workforce Stabilization Bill, for this particular bill is extremely important.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: As a council member from the 42nd Council District, I've had the opportunity to sit down with paraprofessionals from schools throughout my community, and I've heard about the long hours, the dedication they

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: bring to the classrooms, and the sacrifices they make to support our students while working to support their own families.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: These are hardworking New Yorkers paying rent or mortgage and buying groceries, paying for child care, doing everyday things that help build this city and make it bigger, brighter, and better.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Everyday New Yorkers that walk into our schools ready to

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Support our children who need a little extra patience and encouragement and care.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: They celebrate our students'successes, help them overcome challenges, and often become some of the most trusted adults in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: That's why this bill matters.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It sends a clear message that the city values the work of our paraprofessionals who do the big, amazing jobs every day.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When we invest in our paraprofessionals, we're investing in our students and our schools and our communities.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And let me be clear, this legislation does not replace or weaken the collective bargaining process.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It is not a substitute for the work that happens at the bargaining table.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: This is a one-time payment that recognizes the extraordinary commitment of our paraprofessionals while those broader conversations continue.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: We have a responsibility to look out for the people who look out for our children, and today we have the opportunity to do just that.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: I'm proud to support Intro 692, and I hope to see it will cross the finish line today because our paraprofessionals have earned more than our gratitude.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Let's continue as we do today to put some respect on that check.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilman Banks.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You have a vote, Councilmember?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, sir.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Loud and clear.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I forgot to mention, to disclose on the record, that my sister works for the Parks Department.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I vote aye.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I teach at CUNY, and I do want to thank the commission that looked at the Pace Galleries, particularly Carl Weisbrad, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Lillian Paoli-Barrios, and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Larry and Angelo.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They really are the best of New York in terms of their knowledge and city experience, and I want to thank them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I said I.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I work and vote and hang out at CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I was very clear.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Aguero.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I got it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I got it.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Council Member Selvena Brooks-Powers shares her deep personal connection as a proud auntie of an autistic child who depends on paraprofessionals daily. She commends Council Member De La Rosa, the labor chair, and the Speaker for moving the bill forward, and votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Brooks-Powers explains vote on 692A, personal connection as auntie of autistic child

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Brooks Powers.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I just want to echo the sentiments of my colleagues in terms of my deep, deep pleasure in being able to cast a vote for Intro 692A.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As a proud auntie of an autistic miracle child who depends each day on paraprofessionals, I've been able to see firsthand just how critical they are to the nurturing and development of our most valued

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: gems in our life, and that's our children.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so while, as has been said, this is a first step,

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm so thankful that we are serving in a body that are putting people first in terms of attacking the affordability issue and making sure people have wages or

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: funding to be able to have a better quality of life.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And so I commend the bill sponsor, Councilmember De La Rosa, the chair for labor, as well as the speaker for moving this forward.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And with that, I vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Come on.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I vote aye on all with the exception of intro 913 on which I vote no.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Carmen De La Rosa strongly supports the 511 West 171st Street ANCP application, which will allow tenants to buy their apartments for $250 and build generational wealth. On 692A, she thanks the Speaker, UFT, and colleagues, details the $32,000 starting salary and $54,000 after 15 years, notes 1,600-3,000 vacancies, and urges remaining council members to co-sponsor.
chapters.title: De La Rosa explains vote on 511 West 171st St co-op and 692A

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: A moment to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: One moment on 511 West 171st Street.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I just want to say I strongly support this application.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It will create cooperative home ownership for existing tenants of a building that have been working really hard to make sure that they can uplift this building and own their apartments.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: They'll be able to buy their apartments for $250.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So that is building generational wealth.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And the vacant apartments will sell at 80% AMI.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And so that is what it means to truly keep communities together and to be able to build generational wealth and equity in communities.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: In terms of 692A, I want to thank Speaker Menon for her leadership and her vision and for staying true in this process.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to thank the UFT for their strong support, the paras who have led this work.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Obviously, Chair Aldeball for her leadership, Chair Dinowitz for his leadership, and everyone else who was a part of making sure we could get here today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Shout out to Assemblymember Keith Powers, who was the original OG introducer of this bill.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: I want to talk about the numbers real quick.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: $32,000 a year is insufficient.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: After 15 years, they make $54,000 a year in the most expensive city.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: in the country.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: $1.5 billion on Carter cases.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: This bill will provide $10,000 in a one-time stabilization payment so that we can retain this workforce.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: 25,000 paraprofessionals in New York City helping the most vulnerable children.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And right now there's from 1,600 to 3,000 vacancies in this workforce.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: And finally, to the 47 council members who have signed on to this bill, thank you for your support.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We have the Manhattan, the Bronx, the Queensborough presidents.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: It's not too late for Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Come on over, as well as the other four council members.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: We have solid support for this legislation because we recognize that this is an equity issue.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Workers in our city must not be facing eviction and having to pick up food at a food pantry when they're helping our most vulnerable New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: So thank you all for the support.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilman, do you have also disclosures by any chance?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: Say it again.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you have disclosures to make today?

utterances.speaker: Carmen N. De La Rosa
utterances.text: No disclosures, and I vote aye or no.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Eric Dinowitz discloses his affiliation with CUNY (Hunter). He delivers an extended statement supporting Intro 692A, describing paraprofessionals as the people who care most for the most vulnerable students and arguing the Council must demonstrate gratitude through action, not just words. He votes an emphatic yes on all items.
chapters.title: Dinowitz discloses CUNY affiliation, explains vote on 692A

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Denowitz.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'd like to disclose on the record that we're funding CUNY and the transparency resolution, and while I don't hang out too much,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: at Hunter like Gail does, I am affiliated with that institution.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to thank Councilmember De La Rosa, Speaker Menin, Chair Aldebal, for moving the paralegislative

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: forward.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Being in a classroom with students with disabilities, the people who care most oftentimes for the most vulnerable students are our paraprofessionals.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The ones who are there day in and day out, the kids who have the most significant needs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Are the ones with power professionals, and they do just such incredible work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I would always say thank you, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Because that's what we can do.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But as a city council member, we don't just have to say thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We can demonstrate our gratitude.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And today we demonstrate that gratitude through this legislation, through the respect check, through recognizing the dignity in their work, through recognizing their work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And addressing, beginning to address the inequities in the pay that they receive.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And as elected officials, we talk all the time about supporting our students.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But we cannot support our students without supporting the people who do the work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We have to support the people who support our students in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Those are our paraprofessionals.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I am an emphatic yes on this legislation, on all pieces of legislation on today's agenda.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I once again want to thank

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember De La Rosa, Chair Aldebol, and of course,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Speaker, and for really moving forward this really critical piece of legislation for our powers and for our students.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Encarnación.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I vote aye or no.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Epstein.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein thanks De La Rosa, the Speaker, and Chair Aldebol. He shares that paras were critical to his own children's education, calls $32,000/year unlivable, and calls the bill a wise investment. He also supports the elected official salary increase, noting council pay is below nonprofit salaries from over a decade ago.
chapters.title: Epstein explains vote on 692A and elected official salary increase

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I really also want to thank Councilman De La Rosa, our speaker, Chair Audubon.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You think about it, you know, I went to public school, my kids went to public school, and

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: In those classes, the paraprofessionals change lives.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know for my kids who struggled with learning disabilities, the paras were critical to their education.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And to say to New Yorkers that you can live on $32,000 a year

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: is beyond belief.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: No one here, no one in this city thinks anyone who lives in any of our districts can survive in this great city of ours on 32,000.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is a small step in the direction to say to the paras and to the UFT that we see you, that we care about you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We want you to stay in this profession.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The idea that we either have 1,600 or 3,000 vacancies because people can't afford to take these jobs just continues to mean that we're spending more money someplace else.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is a wise investment in our city, a wise investment in our power professionals, and a wise investment in our future.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I really want to applaud us all.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: For taking this stance today.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to applaud us all for taking the leadership, of course, with Speaker of Menon leading all the way, Carmen De La Rosa, and my assembly member, Keith Powers, that we wouldn't be here today with them and the powers who stand up every day.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I vote aye, and I want to also, just one last message around the salary increase, I think.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's you know we need to make sure that leaders in our city want these jobs and salaries That we get paid are below even nonprofit salaries like when I even

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I got paid when I was in a nonprofit leader I got paid more than ten years ago that I get paid today

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And if we really want these people in our profession to make our city better, we need to do it by understanding that salaries mean something to people doing these jobs.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I really applaud the commission and the work we did and what we're doing today on that vote.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Farias.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'd probably vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Felder.

utterances.speaker: Simcha Felder
utterances.text: I vote aye on all, no on intro 972.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Oswald Feliz expresses pride in supporting Intro 913A by Council Member Farias, which raises the maximum age for the NYPD civil service exam from 35 to 43. He notes progress on police headcount and calls the bill a way to expand the candidate pool. He also congratulates De La Rosa on the para bill.
chapters.title: Feliz explains vote on 913A, raising NYPD civil service exam age

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Feliz.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Permission to take to my vote.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Proud to vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: I'm especially proud to support intro 913A by Councilmember Farias.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: 913A, which would raise the age limit for those who would like to take the civil service exam to become a police officer.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Our police department plays an essential role on the issue of public safety, and we must ensure that they have the tools to be able to successfully carry out their important mission.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Now, we've made progress on the issue of police headcount.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We've made progress by removing barriers that get in the way of those who want to serve our city as a police officer.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: We've made progress, and there's still room to take the progress even further.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: This legislation will allow us to do exactly that by expanding the pool of candidates allowed to take the required exams.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: So I'm proud to vote aye on all, including 913A.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: And also, congratulations, Carmen De La Rosa, on your bill as well.

utterances.speaker: Oswald J. Feliz
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kamillah Hanks highlights two projects in her district: a Curtis High School expansion with a nursing program and the 198-208 Richmond Terrace development with 117 apartments including 30 affordable workforce units. utterances.text: The North Shore of Staten Island is changing, and with that change, it becomes a responsibility to make sure that we're approving projects that invest in the future of homes and the future of careers where families can thrive.

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks
utterances.text: And two, I am proud to be voting in support of 198-208 Richmond Terrace, which will bring 117

utterances.speaker: Kamillah Hanks

chapters.summary: Council Member Farah Louis explains her support for Intro 692, calling paraprofessionals the backbone of public schools who provide individualized attention to children who need it most. She thanks the UFT, Speaker Menin, and Council Member De La Rosa. She discloses her brother works for the MTA and votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Lewis explains vote on 692A, discloses brother at MTA

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I want to take a moment to support Intro 692 today, which provides workforce stabilization payments for our school paraprofessionals.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For far too long, paraprofessionals have been the backbone of our public schools without receiving the recognition or compensation that reflects the essential role they play every single day.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: They support students with disabilities, assist teachers in managing classrooms, provide individualized attention to children who need it the most.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: As we continue to face staffing shortages across our education system, retaining experienced paraprofessionals is critical.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: These workforce stabilization payments acknowledge the invaluable work paraprofessionals perform and represent an important step towards building a stronger and more equitable education system for our students and families.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I want to thank the UFT for their advocacy, Speaker Menon and Councilmember De La Rosa for advancing this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I vote aye on all, and I would like to disclose for the record that my brother works for the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Maloney discloses her mother teaches at Hunter College. She expresses pride in co-sponsoring Intro 692 for the para respect check and excitement for Intro 955 making it easier to open small businesses. She urges colleagues to sign onto Intro 875, the stop clock bill, and recognizes summer interns Dylan, Jade, and Nathan. Abreu reminds her to speak only on items being voted on.
chapters.title: Maloney discloses mother at Hunter, explains vote on 692 and 955, recognizes interns

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First, I want to disclose for the record that my mother is a teacher at Hunter.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I vote aye on all, and I'm especially proud to co-sponsor and vote yes on Intro 692, which recognizes the critical role that our school paraprofessionals play in supporting students across the city.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The para-respect check is an important step forward towards providing paraprofessionals with the dignity, compensation, and respect they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I'm also excited to vote for Intro 955, which...

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: makes it easier and faster for New Yorkers to open a small business.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues to sign on to a similar bill, Intro 875, the stop clock bill, which focuses on transparency and accountability on permit timelines.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Apologies.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: No problem.

chapters.summary: Council Member Christopher Marte thanks De La Rosa and the Speaker on the para bill, and thanks his community for organizing and petitioning to landmark St. Mary's Church on Grand Street. utterances.text: Marte.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: First, I want to give a huge thank you to Councilmember De La Rosa and Speaker Menin on the paraprofessional bills.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I also want to give a huge thank you to my community for starting and organizing and petitioning to landmark St.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: So I want to give a huge thank you to Norma Ramirez, Sandra Stothers, who's the TA president at Grand Street Guild,

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
