chapters.summary: Council Member Frank Morano votes yes enthusiastically on Intro 692, congratulating De La Rosa and recognizing former Council Member Keith Powers. He notes regret that a para from his district, Ellen Santarelli, retired the day before and won't receive the benefit, wishes the payment were pensionable, and criticizes the administration's opposition. He votes no on Intro 972 and 983A.
chapters.title: Morano explains vote on 692A, notes para who retired before benefit

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Morano.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I wanted to speak a moment on Intro 692.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'll be voting yes enthusiastically on this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to congratulate Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa for getting this bill across the finish line.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also want to recognize former Councilmember Keith Powers, whose leadership on this issue helped lay the groundwork for where we are today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I don't know that anybody told him he's a former council member, though, because I feel like I see him at City Hall just as much now as when he served in this body.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: He's kind of like the municipal equivalent of Bruce Willis in The Sixth Sense, but it's great to see him.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Every day, our school paraprofessionals do some of the hardest and most important work in our education system.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They help children with disabilities learn, communicate, and succeed.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They support our teachers, strengthen classrooms, and make a real difference in the lives of New York families.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Yet for far too long, they've been asked to do that work for far too little pay.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I do have one regret in this bill, though.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: A wonderful paraprofessional from my district, Ellen Santarelli, devoted her career to serving our children.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: She retired yesterday, and because of the timing, she won't receive this benefit.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That's unfortunate because people like Ellen are...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: are exactly the public servants struggling to afford to continue to live here that we should be rewarding.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also wish this $10,000 payment could be pensionable.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Unfortunately, this legislation doesn't permit that.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: My hope is that making compensation like this pensionable becomes part of the next collective bargaining agreement so these hardworking employees receive not only immediate relief, but greater retirement security as well.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Finally, I think it's disappointing that this administration has indicated its opposition to this and other similar legislation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Too often when it comes to giving working people the support they've earned, whether it's paraprofessionals, EMS workers, or others, we're told that collective bargaining means nothing can be done.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Collective bargaining is obviously important, and it must be respected, but it shouldn't become an excuse for not helping working folks in our city.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: With that, I'll vote aye on everything with the exception of Intro 972 and 983A, on which I vote no.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Mercedes Narcisse discloses her son works for a funded nonprofit and her husband is a doctor at NYU. utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They are very special to us and let's show it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Let's continue making sure that they get what they deserve in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler explains he would have preferred resolving para compensation through collective bargaining, but pattern bargaining is failing the lowest-paid workers. He notes a 3% raise means little at $32,500/year and calls on OLR and the Mamdani administration to create equity tables prioritizing low-wage workers. He votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Restler explains vote on 692A, critiques pattern bargaining

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Rustler.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I, like the Speaker mentioned, would have strongly preferred for us to be resolving compensation for paraprofessionals through the collective bargaining process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But it has become unfortunately apparent over many years that pattern bargaining is failing the lowest paid workers in city government.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are too many workers like paraprofessionals who earn $32,500 a year, where a 3% raise just doesn't mean much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If you're well compensated and you're making over $100,000 a year, a 3% raise is great.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But the nature of pattern bargaining...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Really expands upon, exacerbates the inequities that exist in compensation in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so we need OLR and the Mamdani administration to begin creating equity tables where each year they identify low-wage workers in city government that are underpaid and prioritize bringing those wages up.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But until that happens, we can't afford not to act.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so I'm grateful for the leadership of our former labor chair, Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa, for ensuring that these paraprofessionals get the support that they deserve and appreciate Speaker Menon's leadership and the council today,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: along with the UFT, in delivering for low-wage workers who deserve this and so much more.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm grateful to every paraprofessional in New York City for their hard work in taking care of our kids.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I proudly vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Riley.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Just want to state for the record that my children are scholars at New York City Public School, and we are also members of the YMCA, and I vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Salam.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Just want to vote, I mean, say for the record that my family are members of the YMCA, and I'm proud to vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Justin Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I'd like to disclose I have nothing to disclose.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Pierina Sanchez discloses her sibling works at CUNY. She congratulates De La Rosa and the Speaker on Intro 692, agrees with Ariola that EMTs also deserve more, noting NYPD patrol cars are transporting people to hospitals in her district due to EMT shortages. She highlights that median income in her district is $25,000/year and calls for taxing the rich.
chapters.title: Pierina Sanchez discloses sibling at CUNY, calls for EMT pay equity

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Parina Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'd like to disclose on the record that my sibling works at CUNY, and permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to say congratulations to Councilmember De La Rosa and the Speaker for your leadership on Intro 692.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: $32,000 is not enough.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Our paras deserve more, and I want to agree with Councilmember Areola that our EMTs absolutely do too.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In my district, the way that the shortfall of our EMTs shows up is that NYPD patrol cars are taking people to hospitals every single day instead of EMTs.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I hear the uproar, and I agree.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And I also remind my colleagues that there are so many New York cities within our city.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In my district, the median income is $25,000 a year for a worker.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so $32,000 is not enough for our paras.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: $25,000 a year is not enough for folks in my district.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So let's please continue to fight our inequalities, expand opportunity, and tax the rich so we can fund the key initiatives that can get us there.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I am all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Santos also.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I am all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lynn Schulman calls paraprofessionals the backbone of classrooms who support students with special needs. She says Intro 692A corrects wage disparities created by years of pattern bargaining and sends a clear message that the city cannot deliver quality education without investing in the people who make it possible. She votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Shulman explains vote on 692A, paraprofessionals as backbone of classrooms

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Paraprofessionals are the backbone of our classroom, supporting students with special needs, assisting teachers, and keeping our schools safe and inclusive.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yet for too long, their pay has not reflected the essential role they play.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Intro 692A is about respect, equity, and justice in correcting the wage disparities created by years of pattern bargaining that have left paraprofessionals behind.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This bill recognizes the value of your work and sends a clear message.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: New York City cannot deliver quality education without investing in the people who make it possible.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to congratulate Carmen De La Rosa and Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And by the way, this isn't the first piece of important legislation that has had new life in this session.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I am a proud co-sponsor of this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I was also the last go-round, and with that, I vote aye on all.

chapters.summary: Council Member Althea Stevens associates her remarks with Pierina Sanchez's, highlighting that average income in her district is $36,000/year. She calls for the same energy for nonprofit workers, whose average income is about $40,000, and urges the Council to continue prioritizing all workers struggling with low wages. She votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Stevens explains vote, calls for nonprofit worker pay equity

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: One, I would like to be associated with the remarks of Councilmember Sanchez and also would like to uplift at the same time, in addition to EMS and us having very low wages.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: in our district, my average being at $36,000 a year.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Also, I would like to highlight nonprofit workers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, today, hearing everyone just speak about how it is a disgrace that private professionals are being paid $32,000.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I would love and hope that the same energy continues for nonprofit workers who work tirelessly in jobs that are honestly unfaithless.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And although I know Epstein said he was making more as a nonprofit, that is typically not the case, where most nonprofit workers'average income is about $40,000, who are doing all the work that we all glorify and uplift here.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I just want to continue to say shout out to Councilman De La Rosa for getting us across the field, but also just making sure that we as a council continue to prioritize all workers who are struggling and at the bottom and who are making very low wages

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: than all other New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so thinking about how do we continue to push forward legislation and supporting those who are in most need in all these communities.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shanel Thomas-Henry discloses her daughter participates at Jamaica Center for Arts and Learning. She acknowledges a constituent and family friend, Ms. Gina Lewis, a paraprofessional who introduced her to the bill before she was elected. She thanks De La Rosa and the Speaker and votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry discloses daughter at Jamaica Center, explains vote on 692A

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'd like to disclose that my daughter is a participant at Jamaica Center for Arts and Learning.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'd like to acknowledge a constituent, someone who has known me probably since I was three years old and has always been a close family friend and a community auntie who is a para.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And who actually introduced me to this bill as an advocate even before I was elected.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So I want to say we're bringing it home.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Gina Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you to my kindly Councilmember De La Rosa for carrying this bill and to Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And I vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Ung.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Aionol.

chapters.summary: Council Member Inna Vernikov moves for unanimous consent to vote on land use call-ups and general orders, which is granted. She votes aye on all except Intro 972 and Intro 983A, on which she votes no.
chapters.title: Vernikov votes aye except 972/983A, requests to vote on land use call-ups

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Vernikov.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I move for unanimous consent to vote on all land use call-ups as well as a couple of general orders.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Seeing no objections.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Aye and all with the exception of intro 972 and 983A.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: In which I vote no.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Nantasha Williams discloses her CUNY affiliation. She explains her support for the Quadrennial Advisory Commission recommendations on elected official compensation as a transparent process, and for Intro 496A acknowledging the 1863 draft riots. She thanks her constituent Nia Clark and Cheryl Wills of NY1 for renewed attention. She also shares that her staffer Kate was formerly a para.
chapters.title: Williams discloses CUNY, explains vote on quadrennial commission and draft riots study

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Williams.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I am disclosing on the record that I am affiliated with CUNY, and I'd like permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: One of the greatest responsibilities we have as legislators is to build public trust.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Sometimes that means creating transparent processes for the decisions government makes.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Other times it means taking an honest look at the history that has shaped our city.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Today we're doing both.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Legislation implementing the recommendations of the Quadrennial Advisory Commission that we thank reflects this council's commitment to a transparent, independent, and predictable process for reviewing elected officials'compensation.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Government is strongest when New Yorkers understand not only the decisions we make, but the process behind them.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We're also taking an important step toward acknowledging one of the darkest chapters in New York City's history, the draft riots of 1863.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Included horrific acts of racial violence against black New Yorkers, yet there remains no formal public acknowledgement of those events in our city's landscape.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I want to thank

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: My constituent, Nia Clark, who's reporting on Cheryl Wills, New York One, helped bring renewed attention to the draft riots.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I want to thank Speaker Julie-Menin for her leadership, council staff, committee members, and chairs for their work on these measures, and all of my colleagues for their partnership.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And very briefly in my 53 seconds, as I was just sharing with the speaker that I have a staffer, my special assistant, Kate, who started working for me.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: No, this is, I can do this now.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: It's during general discussion.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: No, this is not my interns.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: This is about the parabola.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: My apologies.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Y'all can all laugh at me now.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You're 10 seconds back, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Yeah, please give her her seconds back.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: That's my apologies.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: You're the best.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Very quickly, Kate, my special assistant, started working for me.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: She's a constituent, part-time.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: When I first got into the council, she eventually moved to full-time.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: She was a para, and one of the things that she mentioned was the salary, and I was able to give her a higher salary.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And perhaps if her salary was higher, sadly, I might not have her in our office.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I'm thankful, but I'm just really excited for this bill and for people like Kate.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And congrats, Chair De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wilson speaks proudly about District 3 as a beacon for dreamers and highlights the Lithuanian Alliance Building in Chelsea, whose landmark designation he supports. He describes how the building served as a first port of call for newly arrived Lithuanian Americans, offering housing and employment assistance. He welcomes the Consul General of Lithuania and votes aye on all.
chapters.title: Wilson explains vote on Lithuanian Alliance building landmark designation

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: I'm proud that District 3 is a beacon for dreamers and strivers from around the nation and the world, to young actors aspiring to make it on Broadway, to queer people yearning for a chosen family, and to visitors taking in the neon lights of Times Square.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: And that's long been the case in District 3.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: Take the Lithuanian Alliance Building in Chelsea, whose landmark designation I am proud to support today.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: From the late 19th through 20th centuries, after making it through Ellis Island, the first port of call for newly arrived Lithuanian Americans was 307 West 30th Street.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: There, they'd get a friendly welcome, assistance with housing and employment, and maybe a copy of the Teveni newspaper printed on site.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: The Lithuanian Alliance building inspires us to extend a hand to newcomers with warmth, hospitality, and professionalism.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: It also represents the ever-important bonds of friendship, dragiste, between Lithuania and the United States.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: I'd like to acknowledge the Council General of Lithuania, as well as the staff members and board members of the Alliance building.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: And I'd like to thank my colleagues for joining me in supporting the landmark designation for the Lithuanian Alliance Center.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: Sveskime kartu.

utterances.speaker: Wilson
utterances.text: And I vote aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: One.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I just want to publicly disclose that my children are, my children are,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: One child is part of a public school system, and I vote aye on all.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong, noting many constituents live paycheck to paycheck and that he advocated for budget savings, says he cannot consciously vote to increase his own salary. He votes no on Intro 983A and no on Intro 972, which would increase city taxes. He votes aye on all other items.
chapters.title: Wong explains vote against 983A salary increase and 972 tax credit

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairman, Leader.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: In my district, there are so, so many constituents that are living paycheck to paycheck and having problems making ends meet, and I just...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: With that in mind, and also I spent many months advocating for savings into the city budget of identifying savings, so I cannot consciously vote on a bill to increase salaries for myself.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I vote I and O except for Intro 983A, and I also vote no on 972 on the tax credit decrease, which would actually increase city taxes.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Joann Ariola, in a second explanation, celebrates Intro 955A as a big win for small business owners who are the backbone of NYC's economy. She describes how the bill coordinates inspections and plan reviews across agencies, cites the prior New Business Acceleration Team model, and notes the bill requires public reporting for accountability. She votes aye on all except 983A.
chapters.title: Ariola explains vote on 955A Red Tape Relief Act for small businesses

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Joann.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: For the record, my kids, my two kids go to public school, which we're funding in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And also, I want to have permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: First of all, I want to congratulate to Paris and also

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: congratulate to our small business owners because today we are voting on a bill, intro 955A, the Ray Tape Relief Act.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: That's a big win for our small business owners because

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: First of all, they are the backbone of New York City's economics.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They create jobs, strengthen our neighborhood, and they represent the dream of hardworking families.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: But too many regulations, too many permits they have to get before they get that door open, even make a penny.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Before they make any penny.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So this bill in 955A will offer a particular solution.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It requires the city agency to coordinate inspections and the plan review instead of making business owners navigate one agency after another.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We know this approach works.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Works because New York City

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: have done it before through new business acceleration team, which helped the business open faster by improving coordination cross agency.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Intro 955A also ensure accountability.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: This legislation requires public

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: reporting so we can measure whether this program is actually reducing the delay and helping business success.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And also I want to thank our speaker and a lot of colleagues helped me to work on this bill and support this bill.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Our job is to make sure the government is more efficient and also make our small business owners'life easier.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So I want to thank everyone who votes yes on this bill.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And also I will vote yes on the bill.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I vote aye on all except the intro 983A, which is no.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Carr.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I'd like to second the comments of colleagues in support of our EMTs, and I vote aye on all with the exception of introductions 972 and 983A, on which I vote no.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Abrayu.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I vote aye on all except pre-considered interim 983A on which I abstain.
