chapters.summary: Council Member Yusef Salaam speaks in support of Resolution 371, declaring August 6 as Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day. He describes Fat Man Scoop (Isaac Freeman III) as a Harlem-raised artist, Grammy winner, and his personal friend who showed up at his inauguration. Salaam notes Scoop died on stage at 56 on August 30, 2024, and asks colleagues to vote yes.
chapters.title: Salaam honors Fat Man Scoop, introduces Resolution 371

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader Abreu.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Colleagues, I want to take a moment before we vote on Resolution 371, because this one deserves more than just a procedural eye.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: First, I want to thank Council Members Farias, Lewis, Farias,

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I'm mixing it up.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Farias, Lewis, Narcisse, and Wilson for supporting this resolution.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And thank you to Deputy Speaker Williams for holding the vote in the committee.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Fat Man's school, known as Isaac Freeman III, was born right here in Manhattan on August 6, 1968.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Harlem raised him, Cardinal Hayes shaped him.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And then he went out and gave this city and this world something they will never forget.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: That voice, that energy, that call and response that turned every room he walked into into a celebration.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: For a decade, he held down midnight to 5 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Slots on Hot 97.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: He collaborated with some of the greatest artists of a generation.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: He won a Grammy, crossed genres, crossed continents, and everywhere he went, people felt it.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: But I want my colleagues to know something beyond the accolades.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: He was my friend.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Oh, that was quick.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'll give you an extra 15 seconds, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Oh, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And I'm going to be consistent with everybody else today.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: As you notice, I'm giving everybody an extra 10 seconds.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: When I was inaugurated, he came and pulled up.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: He didn't have to do that.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Nobody asked him to do it.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: But that was Isaac.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: He showed up for the people he loved the same way that he showed up for every audience with everything he had.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Every single time.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: We lost him on August 30th of 2024 at 56 years of age on stage doing exactly what he was born to do.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: This resolution asks that every August 6th, the city- Council member, you have to wrap up.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Yes, sir.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: The city of New York pause and remember him, not just as an artist, a son of Harlem, as someone who made this city.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Felt like home for everyone who heard his voice.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: And I ask my colleagues to vote yes on Resolution 371.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Councilmember Lewis.

chapters.summary: Council Member Farah Louis shares that Fat Man Scoop was her boss at Hot 97, where she pulled his press clips and broke news stories. She describes how he encouraged her journalism ambitions, pushed her to keep fighting, and taught her to use equipment. She proudly votes in support of the resolution.
chapters.title: Lewis shares personal connection working for Fat Man Scoop at Hot 97

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I would like to thank Councilmember Salam for sponsoring Reso 371 and for giving us the opportunity to honor a man who meant so much to so many.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For most people, Fat Man Scoop was the voice, the booming, raw, unmistakable energy that could fill any room, any club, any track he touched.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: He was the ultimate hip-hop hype man, and he did it with joy every single time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: But for me, Fat Man Scoop was something more personal.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: He was my boss.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I had the privilege of working for him at Hot 97, pulling his press clips every morning, breaking news stories, and making sure he had everything he needed to walk into that studio ready.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And he was always ready.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: What I didn't expect was how much he would pour into me.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: He saw my ambition to be a journalist and to work in media.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And he didn't just encourage me, he pushed me.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: He told me to keep fighting for where I needed to be.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: He showed me how to use the equipment.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Wow, that was fast.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: With all that said, I'm proud to vote in support for this resolution designating August 6th as Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day in the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All right, seeing no one else signed up to speak on resolutions.

chapters.summary: Abreu conducts a voice vote on Resolution 371, declaring August 6 annually as Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day in the City of New York. The ayes have it.
chapters.title: Voice vote on Resolution 371: Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: We will now have a voice vote on today's resos.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: If you wish to vote against or abstain from today's resolution, please notify the legislative documents unit by email or by approaching the dais.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I will now read today's resolution to the record.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Resolution 371 declares August 6th annually as Fat Man Scoop Recognition Day in the city of New York and celebrates the signature style that he brought to every musical collaboration and the inimitable energy he brought to every audience.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: Abreu provides a final revision of the Intro 983A vote, now reported as 42 in the affirmative, 6 in the negative, and 1 abstention.
chapters.title: Final correction: Intro 983A adopted 42-6-1

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And I would also like to also provide a revision of the vote of Intro 983A, which was adopted by a vote of 42 in the affirmative, 6 in the negative, and 1 abstention.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: We will now move into general discussion, beginning with Deputy Speaker Williams, followed by Lewis.

chapters.summary: Council Member Nantasha Williams gives a shout-out to her District 27 summer interns Avani, Ryan, Maribel, Eric, and Owen, thanking them for their hard work, curiosity, and commitment to public service.
chapters.title: Williams recognizes D27 summer interns

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Following the rules, I just want to give a special shout out to my D27 summer interns, Avani, Ryan, Maribel, Eric, and Owen, who are joining us in the chamber today.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your hard work, your curiosity, and your commitment to public service.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I am so grateful to have you with us this summer.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Congrats, interns.

chapters.summary: Council Member Farah Louis introduces Intro 978, which would increase the qualifying income limit for seniors and persons with disabilities for SCRIE and DRIE programs. She cites Manhattan median rent of $5,295 and Brooklyn $4,350, and a report of 26,000 applications for just 84 affordable senior units, urging colleagues to support the bill.
chapters.title: Lewis introduces Intro 978 on SCRIE/DRIE income limits

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Lewis, followed by Wong.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Today I'm introducing Intro 978, a bill that will increase the qualifying income limit for seniors and persons with disabilities for the senior citizen rent increase exemption and the disability screen injury.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This bill is imperative to protect our most vulnerable populations from increasing housing costs and prevent displacement.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: New York City faces a severe housing crisis, which is exacerbated by rent increases that threaten housing stability for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Our seniors and neighbors with disabilities rely heavily on SCRI and DRI because many live on fixed incomes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: These programs allow our older adults to afford their daily essentials without having to choose between buying groceries, purchasing medicine, and paying for rent.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: As of June 2026, New York City experienced another unprecedented housing price increase, with the median monthly rent being $5,295 in Manhattan compared to $4,350 in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: These escalating rent costs reflect a widening disparity of housing affordability.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Across the city.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We must protect our low-income residents from being priced out of neighborhoods they call home for decades.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This bill addresses the eligibility challenges we face as New Yorkers strengthening housing security for tenants, ultimately lowering tenant turnover for landlords and reducing eviction risk for

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Homelessness is already a widespread issue in New York.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: city and fewer affordable units will only increase shelter populations.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: A recent report showing 26,000 applications were received for just 84 affordable senior housing units in the city.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: It is important that we continue to protect our older adults and uniquely abled New Yorkers from experiencing traumatic displacement.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues to support Intro 978.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong introduces Intro 984, establishing a permanent citywide NYPD street takeover unit, prompted by a dangerous April 18th takeover in his district involving 100 vehicles and a fire between two gas stations. The bill would codify Patrol Borough Queens North's approach, expand it citywide, and require NYPD reporting to the Council.
chapters.title: Wong introduces Intro 984 on NYPD street takeover unit

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Santos Suoso.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Today I'm introducing Intro 984, a legislation to formally establish a permanent citywide NYPD street takeover unit.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: This bill was prompted by the dangerous April 18th takeover at 69th Street and Elliott Avenue in my district, where nearly 100 vehicles blocked the intersection,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: drivers performed stunts around a fire in the Broadway between two gas stations, and individuals damaged a mock police vehicle.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Similar incidents across the city have shown that too often the response comes too late and meaningful prevention is lacking.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Patrol Borough Queens North has already taken an important step by deploying a team to address these incidents.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Intro 984 would codify that approach into law, expand it citywide, establish a unit to monitor publicly available information about planned takeovers and require

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: the NYPD to report to the council so we can measure its effectiveness and ensure proper oversight.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But a specialized unit alone is not enough.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: We also need more police officers assigned to our local precincts so they have the staffing necessary to prevent these incidents.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Respond quickly, and maintain public safety throughout our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I ask my colleagues to sign on to Intro 984 so we can advance a serious, permanent, and accountable solution to this growing public safety threat.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Thomas Henry, followed by Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shanel Thomas-Henry, as Small Business Chair, introduces Resolution 561 calling on utility companies and the Public Service Commission to freeze utility rates. She describes utilities as the operating system of communities and argues families and small businesses cannot absorb another increase. She also acknowledges an SYEP intern and her nephew.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry introduces Resolution 561 on utility rate freeze

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Sanchez will also come back to when she's ready.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: There's a saying we've all heard.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: If you can make it in New York, you can make it anywhere.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: New Yorkers have always understood that this city comes with a price.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: We accept that.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I mean, the New York mindset is often anchored on blind faith that success is just on the other side of grinding harder.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: But there's a thin line between paying a premium to live in New York and being priced out of New York.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Every additional increase moves more families and more small businesses closer to that line.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I believe good government meets people where they are.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And right now, New Yorkers are telling us exactly where they are.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They're making impossible choices between paying bills and putting food on the table.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: As chair of the Committee on Small Business, I hear this every single day.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: For a small business,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: All business owner, utilities are the only way doors stay open.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They aren't a luxury.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They're the operating system of our communities.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The refrigerator keeping food fresh, the salon chair staying occupied, the neighborhood laundromat running another load, the corner bodega keeping its shelves stocked.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: That's why I'm proud to introduce Resolution 561, calling on our utility companies and the Public Service Commission to freeze utility rates for New York City residents.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Families are juggling housing, groceries, child care, and transportation.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Seniors living on fixed incomes have the least room for yet another increase.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Our small business are navigating higher rents, rising insurance costs, and inflation.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Utility bills shouldn't be the breaking point.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: An affordable city isn't built by asking people to absorb one more bill.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Today we're asking for something simple, a pause, a freeze, and a recognition that affordability must remain the center of every decision.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you to my colleagues who have already joined me in supporting this effort.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I encourage the rest of the council to join us.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And I would also like to acknowledge one of my SYEP interns who is in the chamber.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Although he hails from the BX in Riley's, Councilmember Riley's district, he has proven to be an invaluable member of our team.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I also want to acknowledge my nephew who is visiting before starting his freshman year at UT Dallas.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Pierina-Sanchez, followed by Santosuosso.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Also then Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kayla Santosuosso calls on the mayor and police commissioner to cooperate with investigations into sexual misconduct allegations against two successive commanding officers at the NYPD 46th Precinct, one leading to a criminal indictment. She notes a homicide the previous night at a lounge she had previously flagged, commends Commissioner Tisch for firing an officer, and calls for swift action on substantiated misconduct against Black and brown New Yorkers.
chapters.title: Santosuosso calls for accountability at NYPD 46th Precinct

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I make these remarks on behalf of my community.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: In less than a year, serious allegations of sexual misconduct have emerged against two successive commanding officers in the NYPD's 46th Precinct, including allegations that have now led to a criminal indictment.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: This represents an alarming pattern of alleged abuse of power, and I call on the mayor and the police commissioner to cooperate fully with all criminal and civil investigations,

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: ensure a thorough and transparent internal disciplinary process, and act swiftly to restore stability, accountability, and trust within the 46th NYPD command.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: In the context of our challenges, our community is outraged and deeply saddened by these allegations.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Even as crime continues to decline citywide, West Bronx residents continue to endure some of the highest levels of violence in our city, including just last night, a horrific homicide

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: occurred outside of a lounge that I had previously brought to the NYPD 46 precinct commander and had flagged for City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: At such a critical moment, our community cannot afford a leadership vacuum or instability in this precinct.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: I commend NYPD Commissioner Tisch for her swift move to fire the officer accused of attempted rape of a junior officer, but I also want to use this moment

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: to call out that the public also needs to see swift action in the face of substantiated NYPD misconduct, especially in the cases of black men and women killed by the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: The heart of the

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Working people of our communities deserve more from the NYPD and from our city.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: We will continue to demand the highest standards of integrity and accountability in policing alongside sustained investments in the foundations of true community safety.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Santos-Suoso, followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer addresses a horrific July 4th shooting in Coney Island where eight people were shot including children as young as six and seven at a NYCHA courtyard barbecue. She thanks the 60th Precinct for apprehending the shooter and calls on experts and community members to contact her office to collectively solve gun violence in Coney Island.
chapters.title: Brewer on Coney Island July 4th shooting, calls for gun violence action

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just want to take a moment to acknowledge that on July 4th in Coney Island, there was a horrific shooting where a family was celebrating July 4th in a barbecue in a NYCHA courtyard.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Eight people were shot, including two children as young as six and seven years old.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Gun violence is unfortunately becoming all too common in Coney Island, despite whatever the citywide stats may say.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I'm grateful for the 60th precinct for apprehending the shooter.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But this is a problem that we need to solve.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I don't pretend to be an expert in this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I will be convening as many people as I can and experts on this issue to try to attack this and really solve it once and for all.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so this is a call to action for anybody who wants to see gun violence ended in our city, but also in Coney Island.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know, please get in touch with my office.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want to work to collectively end this problem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And my heart goes out to the family that's recovering at this time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Kalani.

chapters.summary: In a second statement, Brewer thanks Council Member Lewis for the SCRIE/DRIE bill and then recognizes her summer interns by name and school, including students from Middlebury, McGill, Barnard, Oberlin, Macaulay Honors, NYU Tisch, Stuyvesant, and Trinity School.
chapters.title: Brewer thanks Lewis for SCRIE/DRIE bill, recognizes summer interns

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do want to thank Councilmember Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: For the screedree, and it's going to make a huge difference for a lot of older adults.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Look forward to working with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I love interns.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank those who are here today.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I respect them, and it's a pleasure to work with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Phoebe Adler, second year at Middlebury College.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Vangelita Benaskis, McGill University.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Zavia Brenner, who's a second year at Barnard College.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Rebecca Chen, who's also second year at Barnard College, and she's a food influencer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Asha Garodnik, Garodnik, sophomore at Trinity School.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Mateo Gregorio, senior at Oberlin College.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Isaac Ho, University of Buffalo.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Julia Hornich, who is at Macaulay Honors College, third year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: James Liu, who is a Van Fellow and also will be getting a second or maybe third degree, the National Sheng-Chi University in Taiwan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Sienna Link-Morris, who's third year at NYU Tisch.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Adam Moss, who's a sophomore at Stuyvesant High School.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Ella Williams at Horace Mann School, and Veronica Hunt, who's a junior at Trinity School.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you all.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's just a few of the interns we have in our office.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I thank each and every one of you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All right, Councilmember Cavan, followed by Morano.

chapters.summary: Council Member Tiffany Cabán echoes calls for swift NYPD action on killings of Black and brown New Yorkers, not just violence against co-workers. She then acknowledges outgoing Progressive Caucus Executive Director Emmett Turan for his thoughtful, driven leadership, noting he is joining Council Member Hanif's staff.
chapters.title: Cabán on NYPD accountability and outgoing Progressive Caucus Director Emmett Turan

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to echo the sentiments of Councilmember Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great to see that the commissioner is willing to swiftly fire an officer who commits an act of abuse of power and violence against a co-worker and would like to see the same swiftness when there are substantiated

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: claims of not just violence against black and brown New Yorkers, but the killings, the murders of black and brown New Yorkers who not only continue to stay on the force, but continue to get raises, to get promoted, to get their pensions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I think it says a lot to say where the action is happening and where it isn't.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: With that, I want to shift gears and take a moment to just acknowledge a New Yorker who has just dedicated so much of himself,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: his time, his energy for a progressive vision, towards a progressive vision that delivers safety and justice and affordability for working people across our city for the last few years.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I, along with so many of others, have had the privilege of working with the now outgoing Progressive Caucus Executive Director,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Emmett Turan, day in and day out, and we're going to miss your thoughtful, driven, and kind leadership as you leave this role, but really, really psyched that you're not going far because he's joining Councilmember Shahana-K.-Hanif's staff and team.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, Emmett, thank you for all you've done for the Progressive Caucus.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can't wait to see what you do next with Councilmember Hanif.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Councilman Murano, followed by Riley.

chapters.summary: Council Member Frank Morano introduces Resolution 555 supporting state legislation for better notice before utility rate hikes, requiring regulators to consider family impacts, strengthening consumer protections, and improving transparency. He frames it as a nonpartisan kitchen table issue affecting homeowners, tenants, and small business owners alike.
chapters.title: Morano introduces Resolution 555 on utility rate transparency

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Today I'm introducing Resolution 555 that speaks to something every one of us has heard about from our constituents, irrespective of party or borough, utility bills that just keep going up.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: For millions of New Yorkers, electricity and natural gas aren't luxuries, they're necessities.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You can postpone buying a new television set, you can't postpone keeping the lights on or refrigerating your food or heating your home in the winter, and yet too many New Yorkers open their utility bill every month and ask the same question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How did it get this high?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This resolution doesn't pretend that the City Council controls utility rates.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We don't, obviously.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That's the responsibility of Albany and the Public Service Commission.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But when New Yorkers are struggling, I think it's our responsibility to speak up.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And the package of state legislation that this resolution supports would provide better notice before rate hikes, require regulators to consider the impact that utility rates have on families, strengthen consumer protections,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: improve payment plan standards, and bring greater transparency and accountability to utility regulations.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: These are not radical ideas at all.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They are common sense, nonpartisan reforms rooted in good government.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Whether you're a homeowner on Staten Island, a tenant in the Bronx, or a small business owner in Brooklyn, everyone deserves to know what they're paying for, why costs.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: are increasing and whether someone is truly looking out for the people paying the bills, not just the entities that are sending them.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This shouldn't be a Republican or Democratic issue, it's a kitchen table issue.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Every dollar a family spends unnecessarily on utility bills is a dollar they can't spend on groceries, rent, prescriptions, or saving for their children's future.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Government can't solve every problem overnight, but we can insist on greater transparency.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We can demand accountability, and we can stand with the people who work hard, pay their bills, and simply want a fair deal.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I hope my colleagues will join me in supporting this resolution and sending a clear message to Albany.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: New Yorkers deserve an energy system that's not only reliable, but affordable, transparent, and worthy of the public's trust.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Riley, followed by Aviles.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kevin Riley introduces Intro 981, establishing a task force to study the feasibility of creating a municipal public bank in New York City. The task force would evaluate governance, capitalization, regulatory compliance, and sustainability, bringing together experts in community banking, labor, and consumer advocacy. He thanks co-prime sponsors and gives a shout-out to an intern.
chapters.title: Riley introduces Intro 981 on municipal public bank task force

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Today I'm proud to introduce Intro 981, legislation that takes an important first step toward exploring how New York City can expand access to responsible, community-focused financial investments.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: This bill establishes a task force to study the feasibility of creating a municipal public bank and to develop recommendations for what such an institution could look like here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Bringing together experts in community banking, small businesses, labor, economic development, and consumer advocacy will ensure this conversation is informed by a broad range of perspectives and grounded in the needs of the communities we serve.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: The task force will evaluate

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: governance, capitalization, financial management, regulatory compliance, and long-term sustainability before issuing a comprehensive report with recommendations to guide future policymaking.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: By requiring a transparent, data-driven process, this legislation ensures that any future decisions are based on thoughtful analysis, stakeholder engagement, and sound financial planning.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: As our city continues to explore new ways to strengthen local economies, support small businesses, and expand access to capital, we have a responsibility to carefully examine every viable option.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: This legislation does exactly that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: It creates a framework for informed decision-making while prioritizing accountability, transparency, and the long-term interests of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: I want to thank Councilmember Joseph, Caban, Stevens, Epstein, and Aviles for co-priming this bill, and I look forward to working with the rest of my colleagues and stakeholders as this conversation continues.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: And with the remaining 20 seconds, I want to give a shout-out to Deputy Speaker's intern that she forgot to mention, Samantha.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: She loves you, okay?

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: That was sweet, I have to say.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Aviles, followed by Denowitz.

chapters.summary: Council Member Alexa Avilés mourns two people killed by ICE agents this month: Joan Sebastian Guerrero, a 26-year-old father killed on his way to work in Maine, and Lorenzo Araujo, a father of three and construction company owner killed while driving co-workers to a job. She notes ICE admitted neither was a target of any investigation, condemns the killings, and calls to abolish ICE.
chapters.title: Avilés mourns killings by ICE, calls to abolish ICE

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: First, I want to thank Emmett Turan, our outgoing Progressive Caucus Director, for his incredible work leading the caucus.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Emmett is a dedicated and supremely talented public service whose thoughtful leadership has delivered major wins for New York's working class.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you, Emmett, for all you have done and will continue to accomplish with Councilmember Hanif's office.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wish to use my remarks to mourn the loss of two people murdered by ICE this month.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Joan Sebastian Guerrero Lorenzo Salgado Araujo.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Joan was a 26-year-old father.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Working two jobs to support his family.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: On Monday morning, ICE agents killed him on his way to work, just blocks from his home.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: He built a community for himself and his family in Maine.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: His neighbor said he was a kind presence on the block, often stopping to compliment a neighbor's garden with his daughter.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Those same neighbors heard screams and cries from his wife and child on Monday when ICE agents shot and killed him in front of their eyes.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Lorenzo Araujo was also headed to work when immigration agents stopped his vehicle.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Lorenzo, a father of three, owned a construction company and he was driving his co-workers to a job when ICE

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: officer stopped his vehicle, pulled out their weapons, and shot and killed him.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: In the days since these murders, ICE officials have admitted that neither Lorenzo or Joan Juan were the target of any investigation.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: This is yet another horrifying instance of ICE's rogue violence and absolute cruelty of the Trump immigration policy.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I stand today to express my utter condemnation of these cold-blooded murders.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: ICE is a lawless agency that has killed at least six people by shooting them to death on our streets since Trump took office.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Again, abolish ICE.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Denowitz, followed by Hanif.

chapters.summary: Council Member Eric Dinowitz introduces Resolution 548 calling on the state legislature to grant the City Council representation on the Panel for Educational Policy and give Community Education Councils more oversight over DOE hiring decisions. He notes the PEP oversees school closures, co-locations, and massive contracts affecting 1 million students, yet no voting member is selected by the Council.
chapters.title: Dinowitz introduces Resolution 548 on school governance reform

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Today, I'm introducing a resolution calling for reform of the New York City public school governance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Resolution 548 calls on the state legislature to amend our city's education law to grant the city council representation on the panel for educational policy

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: and to give community education councils more oversight over DOE hiring decisions.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The PEP wields immense power in our city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They oversee school closures, co-locations, grade reconfigurations, massive DOE contracts, including those exceeding $1 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Their actions affect nearly 1 million students and 140,000 DOE staff members.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And yet, of the PEP's voting members, none are selected by the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The City Council, which oversees and adopts the budget that funds the DOE, does not have any representation on the PEP.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Giving the City Council a seat would help democratize the panel and ensure that our communities are adequately represented in decisions that affect them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Community education councils face a similar imbalance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While CECs help identify candidates for superintendent and submit evaluations, they have no meaningful authority over leadership decisions within their district.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The state should amend the system to allow for CECs, a body that directly represents parents within their school district, to have more of a say in decision making.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Granting the council a desire

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: designated seat on the PEP and giving CECs a stronger role in hiring decisions within their districts would add a necessary measure of legislative and community accountability into the largest public school system in the United States.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you again, Majority Leader, for the opportunity to speak on this resolution.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Hanif, followed by Hudson, and then Justin Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shahana Hanif introduces Intro 971, requiring DCWP to study the impacts of private equity ownership on workers, consumers, and small businesses across industries including healthcare, dental, veterinary, child care, and hospitality. She also marks Disability Pride Month, celebrating the disability community's leadership and recommitting to accessibility and disability justice.
chapters.title: Hanif introduces Intro 971 on private equity study, marks Disability Pride Month

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Today I'm proud to introduce Intro 971, legislation that takes an important first step toward understanding and addressing the growing impact of private equity on our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Our small businesses are already struggling with rising rents, higher operating costs, and burdensome regulations.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Now, many are also competing against private equity-backed companies that can undercut local businesses, prioritize profits over quality,

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: weaken worker protections, and displace the neighborhood institutions that make our communities unique.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Every dollar spent at a local business is an investment in your neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Despite the rapid growth of private equity ownership, the city has little data on how these acquisitions are reshaping our communities.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Intro 971 would require the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection to conduct a one-time study

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: examining the impacts of private equity ownership on workers, consumers, local economies, and small businesses across industries, including health care.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: dental care, veterinary services, child care, food and hospitality, fitness, and more.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Finally, July is Disability Pride Month.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: As chair of the Committee on Disabilities and Accessibility, I want to celebrate the leadership, joy, and resilience of the disability community and recommit.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: To building a city where accessibility, inclusion, and disability justice, disabilities justice are never an afterthought.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Hudson, followed by Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Council Member Crystal Hudson takes a moment to shout out her summer interns serving the 35th District, naming students from Cornell, Touro College, Spelman College, Yeshiva University, Stanford, Rochester Institute of Technology, and York College.
chapters.title: Hudson recognizes summer interns

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'd like to be associated with the remarks of Councilmember Aviles, and I'd also like to take a quick moment to shout out my summer interns who have already made an impact in their short time serving the 35th District alongside my team.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Skylar Silverman at Cornell University, Yaron Berry, a recent graduate of Toro College, Callie Hayes of my own alma mater, Spelman College, Octavia Rubayo-Jenkins at Yeshiva University School of Social Work,

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Jed Kasoy from Stanford University, Clinton Charter, a recent graduate of Rochester Institute of Technology, and Raylene Brown Alexander at York College.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Majority Leader Abreu briefly recognizes his intern and constituent Theo Crombie, who has joined the meeting in the chamber.
chapters.title: Abreu recognizes intern Theo Crombie

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I would also like to take a moment to recognize we've been joined by Theo Crombie in the back, who's an intern of mine and also a constituent.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Council Member Justin Sanchez.

chapters.summary: Council Member Justin Sanchez, celebrating his 35th birthday the next day, reflects on his mother Ruth Sanchez who was a scared 18-year-old when she had him. He thanks her for her struggles and fights, and delivers an inspirational message to Black and brown children across the city, saying this city belongs to them and that New Yorkers never stop.
chapters.title: Justin Sanchez reflects on 35th birthday and mother's sacrifices

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: First of all, thank you, Madam Speaker, and to all my colleagues for the early birthday wishes.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I look forward to entering my 35th year of life tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: But I want to rewind back to today, 35 years ago, where a scared 18-year-old girl was sitting there, pregnant, not knowing what the future would hold.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That girl was my mom, Ruth Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And I want to thank her for everything that she went through, all of the struggles, all of the fights, to be able to make sure that her little boy and all of her kids got the opportunity to live out their wildest dreams.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And 35 years later, I get to enter my 35th birthday as the council member representing the South Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Having said that, this message goes out to all of the little black and brown girls and boys all over this city.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This is your city.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: At every time, the world might seem hard, it might seem unfair, it is there.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Are people who are rooting for you at every single step of the way.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And I'm proud to be here, and I'm so excited to see all the work that we can do together, because at the end of the day, this city belongs to us, and no matter what, the amount of no's that you will get every left and right, at the end of the day, we're New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Nothing stops us, and the only thing getting in your way is you.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So thank you, Mom.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you to this city, and thank you, everyone.

chapters.summary: Abreu calls on Speaker Menin to close the stated meeting. Council staff announces the stated meeting of July 16, 2026 is hereby adjourned.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin closes meeting, adjourned

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I will now call on Speaker Julie-Menin to close today's stated meeting.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Dated meeting of July 16th, 2026 is hereby adjourned.

summary: The Council voted on a packed agenda approving 3,242 new housing units across all five boroughs, landmark designations tied to immigration history, paraprofessional workforce stabilization payments of up to $10,000, elected official salary increases of 18.2%, small business permitting reform, and wellness checks for older adults.

Speaker Menin highlighted the Romange's Law hearing, $1.5M for free swim lessons, and the paraprofessional pay crisis, with 27 council members explaining their votes during an extended general orders roll call.

During general discussion, members introduced new bills on utility rate freezes, NYPD street takeover units, municipal public banking, SCRIE/DRIE income limits, private equity studies, and school governance reform, while honoring Fat Man Scoop with a Recognition Day resolution.
title: Stated Meeting
